reasoning: I will provide a concise, neutral summary of the article, capturing the key points about the pay offer, reactions from UCU and employers, ongoing ballots, planned industrial action, and related context (exams, negotiations on contracts, etc.). I will avoid adding new information or personal opinions.
output: Universities and Colleges Employers Association offered a 1.1% pay rise to staff, which the University and College Union (UCU) condemned as an insult. The UCU argues that staff have suffered about a 14% real-terms pay cut since 2009 while university leaders have benefited from large pay rises, including around 5% for vice-chancellors. Employers say the offer is the best possible given affordability and that the weighting could lift the worst-paid staff by more than 5%; they also offered talks on zero-hours contracts and improving lower pay for female academics. Ballots on the improved offer are under way for Unison and Unite, with both unions recommending rejection, and UCU has rejected the 1.1% offer as only a marginal improvement on the original 1%. Industrial action could take place in the autumn term, with UCU planning strike rallies. Meanwhile, staff are working to contract from Wednesday, refusing extra duties, cover for absent colleagues, or overtime. Summer exams are still ongoing at some universities, with most institutions expecting minimal disruption.
